questionText: Disprove the statement: ``Every even number greater than $2$ can be written as the sum of two \emph{distinct} prime numbers.''

steps.description: Read the claim as a universal statement.
steps.workingLatex: \forall\ \text{even } n > 2:\ n = p + q,\ p \neq q \text{ prime}
steps.explanation: The claim strengthens Goldbach's conjecture by demanding the two primes be *distinct*; one even number needing equal primes disproves it.
We need a single even $n>2$ that cannot be split into two *different* primes.
The smallest even number greater than $2$ is $4$, where few primes are available.
Only the primes $2$ and $3$ are at most $4$, limiting the possible sums.

steps.description: Try to write 4 as a sum of two primes.
steps.explanation: The only way to write $4$ as a sum of two primes is $2+2$.
But the two primes here are both $2$, so they are not distinct, violating the requirement.
The only other splitting $3+1$ fails because $1$ is not prime, so no distinct-prime representation exists.
The even number $4$ genuinely has no representation as a sum of two distinct primes.
Since $4$ requires $2+2$ (not distinct), the strengthened claim is disproved.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Take $n=4$: the only prime sum is $4 = 2 + 2$, whose primes are not distinct. Hence the statement is false. $\blacksquare$
